{"type":"tool_use","timestamp":1781020439299,"sessionID":"ses_152ea6dbfffeVOjKLRCTiJiYk6","part":{"type":"tool","tool":"write","callID":"call_00_TAvoAnvOkqXZjG4xJhbl1377","state":{"status":"completed","input":{"filePath":"/app/analyze.py","content":"#!/usr/bin/env python3\n\"\"\"\nSupply Chain Quality Offender Analysis\n\nThis script parses the supply chain data to identify which producers caused\nconsumer quality rejections for each product.\n\"\"\"\n\nimport csv\nimport json\nimport os\nimport re\nfrom collections import defaultdict\nfrom glob import glob\n\n# ============================================================\n# STEP 1: Parse orders (what stores ordered, per day per product)\n# ============================================================\n\ndef parse_order_file(filepath, day, store):\n    \"\"\"Parse order file in any format and return {product: qty} dict.\"\"\"\n    products = {}\n    content = open(filepath).read()\n    \n    # Try JSON format\n    if filepath.endswith('.json'):\n        try:\n            import json\n            data = json.loads(content)\n            for item in data.get('items', []):\n                pid = item.get('prod_id', item.get('product', ''))\n                qty = int(item.get('qty', item.get('quantity', 0)))\n                if pid:\n                    products[pid] = qty\n            return products\n        except:\n            pass\n    \n    # Try CSV format (PRODUCT;QUANTITY)\n    if filepath.endswith('.csv'):\n        for line in content.strip().split('\\n')[1:]:  # skip header\n            parts = line.strip().split(';')\n            if len(parts) >= 2:\n                pid = parts[0].strip()\n                qty = int(parts[1].strip())\n                products[pid] = qty\n        return products\n    \n    # Try key-value format (RECEIVER: ... | ORDER_PRODUCT: ... | ORDER_QTY: ...)\n    if 'RECEIVER' in content or 'ORDER_PRODUCT' in content or 'ORDER_QTY' in content:\n        for line in content.strip().split('\\n'):\n            m = re.search(r'ORDER_PRODUCT:\\s*(\\S+)', line)\n            m2 = re.search(r'ORDER_QTY:\\s*(\\d+)', line)\n            if m and m2:\n                products[m.group(1)] = int(m2.group(1))\n        return products\n    \n    # Try text-template format (\"Order from Day_X. Please deliver XX units of product P_X.\")\n    for line in content.strip().split('\\n'):\n        m = re.search(r'(\\d+)\\s+units of product\\s+(P_\\d+)', line)\n        if m:\n            products[m.group(2)] = int(m.group(1))\n    \n    return products\n\n\ndef load_all_orders():\n    \"\"\"Load all store orders. Returns dict: day -> store -> product -> qty\"\"\"\n    orders = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(dict))\n    \n    order_files = glob('/app/raw_orders/day_*')\n    for fp in sorted(order_files):\n        fname = os.path.basename(fp)\n        # Format: day_XXX_S_X_*.ext\n        parts = fname.split('_')\n        # Extract day number\n        day = int(parts[1])\n        # Extract store ID\n        store = parts[2] + '_' + parts[3]  # e.g. S_1\n        \n        prods = parse_order_file(fp, day, store)\n        if prods:\n            orders[day][store] = prods\n    \n    return orders\n\n\n# ============================================================\n# STEP 2: Parse wholesaler receipts (which suppliers were accepted)\n# ============================================================\n\ndef load_wholesaler_receipts():\n    \"\"\"\n    Load wholesaler receipts.\n    Returns dict: day -> {producer: sequence_no}\n    \"\"\"\n    receipts = defaultdict(dict)\n    \n    receipt_files = glob('/app/raw_wholesaler_receipts/day_*.txt')\n    for fp in sorted(receipt_files):\n        fname = os.path.basename(fp)\n        # day_XXX_PR_XX_receipt.txt\n        parts = fname.split('_')\n        day = int(parts[1])\n        producer = parts[2] + '_' + parts[3]\n        \n        content = open(fp).read()\n        seq_match = re.search(r'DAILY_SEQUENCE_NO:\\s*(\\d+)', content)\n        if seq_match:\n            seq = int(seq_match.group(1))\n            receipts[day][producer] = seq\n    \n    return receipts\n\n\n# ============================================================\n# STEP 3: Parse supplier receipts (what each producer delivered)\n# ============================================================\n\ndef parse_supplier_receipt_file(filepath, day, producer):\n    \"\"\"Parse supplier receipt file and return {product: qty} dict.\"\"\"\n    products = {}\n    content = open(filepath).read()\n    \n    # Try JSON format\n    if filepath.endswith('.json'):\n        try:\n            import json\n            data = json.loads(content)\n            for item in data.get('items', []):\n                pid = item.get('prod_id', '')\n                qty = int(item.get('delivered_qty', 0))\n                if pid:\n                    products[pid] = qty\n            return products\n        except:\n            pass\n    \n    # Try CSV format (PRODUCT;DELIVERED_QTY)\n    if filepath.endswith('.csv'):\n        for line in content.strip().split('\\n')[1:]:\n            parts = line.strip().split(';')\n            if len(parts) >= 2:\n                pid = parts[0].strip()\n                qty = int(parts[1].strip())\n                products[pid] = qty\n        return products\n    \n    # Try key-value format (PRODUCT: ... | DELIVERED_QTY: ...)\n    if 'PRODUCT' in content and 'DELIVERED_QTY' in content:\n        for line in content.strip().split('\\n'):\n            m = re.search(r'PRODUCT:\\s*(\\S+)', line)\n            m2 = re.search(r'DELIVERED_QTY:\\s*(\\d+)', line)\n            if m and m2:\n                products[m.group(1)] = int(m2.group(1))\n        return products\n    \n    # Try text-template format (\"Supplier PR_XX delivered XX units of product P_X\")\n    for line in content.strip().split('\\n'):\n        m = re.search(r'delivered\\s+(\\d+)\\s+units of product\\s+(P_\\d+)', line)\n        if m:\n            products[m.group(2)] = int(m.group(1))\n    \n    return products\n\n\ndef load_supplier_receipts():\n    \"\"\"\n    Load all supplier receipts.\n    Returns dict: day -> producer -> {product: qty}\n    \"\"\"\n    deliveries = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(dict))\n    \n    files = glob('/app/raw_supplier_receipts/day_*')\n    for fp in sorted(files):\n        fname = os.path.basename(fp)\n        parts = fname.split('_')\n        day = int(parts[1])\n        producer = parts[2] + '_' + parts[3]\n        \n        prods = parse_supplier_receipt_file(fp, day, producer)\n        if prods:\n            deliveries[day][producer] = prods\n    \n    return deliveries\n\n\n# ============================================================\n# STEP 4: Parse wholesaler store receipts\n# ============================================================\n\ndef load_wholesaler_store_receipts():\n    \"\"\"\n    Load wholesaler store receipts.\n    Returns dict: day -> [(store, sequence_no, total_units)]\n    \"\"\"\n    receipts = defaultdict(list)\n    \n    files = glob('/app/raw_wholesaler_store_receipts/day_*.txt')\n    for fp in sorted(files):\n        fname = os.path.basename(fp)\n        parts = fname.split('_')\n        day = int(parts[1])\n        store = parts[2] + '_' + parts[3]\n        \n        content = open(fp).read()\n        seq_match = re.search(r'DAILY_SEQUENCE_NO:\\s*(\\d+)', content)\n        units_match = re.search(r'TOTAL_DELIVERED_UNITS:\\s*(\\d+)', content)\n        \n        if seq_match and units_match:\n            seq = int(seq_match.group(1))\n            units = int(units_match.group(1))\n            receipts[day].append((store, seq, units))\n    \n    # Sort by sequence no for each day\n    for day in receipts:\n        receipts[day].sort(key=lambda x: x[1])\n    \n    return receipts\n\n\n# ============================================================\n# STEP 5: Load sales log\n# ============================================================\n\ndef load_sales_log():\n    \"\"\"\n    Load sales log.\n    Returns dict: day -> store -> product -> [list of (demand, sold, lost)]\n    Also returns aggregated: (day, store, product) -> (total_demand, total_sold, total_lost)\n    \"\"\"\n    daily_sales = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(list)))\n    aggregated = {}\n    \n    with open('/app/sales_log.csv') as f:\n        reader = csv.DictReader(f)\n        for row in reader:\n            day = int(row['Day'])\n            store = row['Store_ID']\n            prod = row['Product_ID']\n            demand = int(row['Reported_Demand'])\n            sold = int(row['Sold_Quantity'])\n            lost = int(row['Lost_Sales'])\n            \n            daily_sales[day][store][prod].append((demand, sold, lost))\n    \n    for day in daily_sales:\n        for store in daily_sales[day]:\n            for prod in daily_sales[day][store]:\n                records = daily_sales[day][store][prod]\n                total_demand = sum(r[0] for r in records)\n                total_sold = sum(r[1] for r in records)\n                total_lost = sum(r[2] for r in records)\n                aggregated[(day, store, prod)] = (total_demand, total_sold, total_lost)\n    \n    return daily_sales, aggregated\n\n\n# ============================================================\n# STEP 6: Supply Chain Simulation\n# ============================================================\n\ndef simulate_supply_chain(orders, wholesaler_receipts, supplier_deliveries, store_receipts, sales_agg):\n    \"\"\"\n    Simulate the supply chain to trace which producer batches reached which stores.\n    \n    The simulation:\n    1. Each day, producers deliver products. Wholesaler accepts them in sequence order.\n    2. Wholesaler accumulates inventory from accepted deliveries.\n    3. Each day, stores send orders. Wholesaler fulfills them in store sequence order.\n    4. Store receives batches (producer source, day received, quantities).\n    5. Sales are recorded.\n    \n    Returns: mapping of (day, store, product) -> list of (producer, source_day, qty) that was in the store's inventory\n    \"\"\"\n    \n    # Track which producer batches are accepted each day by the wholesaler\n    # producer_batches[day] = list of (producer, seq_no, {product: qty})\n    # But we need to process day by day\n    \n    # For each day, determine accepted supplier deliveries\n    accepted_batches = defaultdict(list)  # day -> [(producer, seq_no, {product: qty})]\n    \n    for day in sorted(wholesaler_receipts.keys()):\n        for producer, seq in sorted(wholesaler_receipts[day].items(), key=lambda x: x[1]):\n            if producer in supplier_deliveries.get(day, {}):\n                accepted_batches[day].append((producer, seq, supplier_deliveries[day][producer]))\n    \n    # Wholesaler inventory: product -> [(producer, source_day, remaining_qty)]\n    wholesaler_inv = defaultdict(list)\n    \n    # Store inventory: store -> product -> [(producer, source_day, remaining_qty)]\n    store_inv = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(list))\n    \n    # Store orders that are pending (not fulfilled)\n    pending_orders = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(int))  # store -> product -> qty\n    \n    # Track which producer batches were in which store at which time\n    # store_batch_history: store -> list of (day, product, producer, source_day, qty)\n    store_batch_history = defaultdict(list)\n    \n    # Also track store intake per day\n    store_intake = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(int))))\n    # store_intake[day][store][product][producer] = qty\n    \n    max_day = max(max(orders.keys()), max(store_receipts.keys()), max(wholesaler_receipts.keys()))\n    \n    for day in range(1, max_day + 1):\n        # Step A: Wholesaler accepts deliveries for the day (in sequence order)\n        if day in accepted_batches:\n            for producer, seq, products in accepted_batches[day]:\n                for prod, qty in products.items():\n                    wholesaler_inv[prod].append([producer, day, qty])\n        \n        # Step B: Process store orders for the day\n        day_orders = orders.get(day, {})\n        \n        # Check store_receipts for this day to determine fulfillment order and quantity\n        store_seq_list = store_receipts.get(day, [])\n        \n        if store_seq_list:\n            # We know the total units each store received\n            # We need to allocate these units to specific products based on store orders\n            # and available wholesaler inventory\n            \n            for store, seq, total_units in store_seq_list:\n                if store in day_orders:\n                    store_order = day_orders[store]\n                    \n                    # Determine which products to fulfill based on order priority\n                    # (FIFO on wholesale inventory per product)\n                    # Allocate total_units across the ordered products\n                    \n                    # First calculate total ordered\n                    total_ordered = sum(store_order.values())\n                    \n                    # Determine what quantities to allocate\n                    allocated = {}\n                    ordered_items = sorted(store_order.items())\n                    \n                    remaining_units = total_units\n                    for prod, ordered_qty in ordered_items:\n                        if ordered_qty > 0 and remaining_units > 0:\n                            # How much of this product can we take from wholesaler?\n                            available_in_wh = sum(item[2] for item in wholesaler_inv.get(prod, []))\n                            alloc = min(ordered_qty, available_in_wh, remaining_units)\n                            if alloc > 0:\n                                allocated[prod] = alloc\n                                remaining_units -= alloc\n                    \n                    # Now allocate from wholesaler inventory (FIFO)\n                    for prod, alloc_qty in allocated.items():\n                        remaining_alloc = alloc_qty\n                        new_inv_items = []\n                        for item in wholesaler_inv.get(prod, []):\n                            if remaining_alloc <= 0:\n                                new_inv_items.append(item)\n                            else:\n                                producer, source_day, avail = item\n                                take = min(avail, remaining_alloc)\n                                if take > 0:\n                                    store_intake[day][store][prod][producer] += take\n                                    store_batch_history[store].append((day, prod, producer, source_day, take))\n                                    # Reconstruct daily sales and inventory consumption\n    # Track which batches were consumed each day\n    \n    # For each day, we need to know how much of each product each store sold,\n    # and from which batch (producer) it came from\n    \n    # Let's build a chronological simulation of inventory consumption\n    \n    # store_inv[store][product] = list of [producer, source_day, remaining_qty]\n    inv = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(list))\n    for store in store_inv:\n        for prod in store_inv[store]:\n            for item in store_inv[store][prod]:\n                inv[store][prod].append(item[:])  # deep copy\n    \n    confirmed_by_product = defaultdict(set)\n    \n    # Sort sales by (day, store, product)\n    sales_keys = sorted(sales_agg.keys())\n    \n    # Track consumer quality state: (store, product, consumer_segment) -> recovery_day_left\n    # Actually let's track at the product-store level\n    # demand_multiplier[store][product] = current multiplier (1.0 = normal)\n    quality_recovery = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(float))\n    \n    # For each store and product, track whether there's currently a quality issue\n    for day in range(1, 366):\n        # Before processing sales for this day, check if any batches in inventory\n        # might have quality issues that we detected previously\n        \n        for store in range(1, 11):\n            store_id = f'S_{store}'\n            for prod_idx in range(1, 31):\n                prod_id = f'P_{prod_idx}'\n                key = (day, store_id, prod_id)\n                \n                if key in sales_agg:\n                    total_demand, total_sold, total_lost = sales_agg[key]\n                    \n                    if total_lost > 0 and total_demand > 0:\n                        # There were lost sales. Check if we had inventory.\n                        inv_list = inv.get(store_id, {}).get(prod_id, [])\n                        total_inventory = sum(item[2] for item in inv_list)\n                        \n                        if total_inventory > 0:\n                            # We had inventory but consumers didn't buy -> quality issue!\n                            # The batch at the front of FIFO is the culprit\n                            front_batch = inv_list[0] if inv_list else None\n                            if front_batch and front_batch[2] > 0:\n                                producer = front_batch[0]\n                                confirmed_by_product[prod_id].add(producer)\n                    \n                    # Consume inventory (simulate selling)\n new_inv_list.append([producer, source_day, avail - take])\n                    inv[store_id][prod_id] = new_inv_list\n    \n    return confirmed_by_product\n\n\n# v in store_receipts.values())} store receipts\")\n    \n    print(\"Loading sales log...\")\n    sales_daily, sales_agg = load_sales_log()\n    print(f\"  Loaded {len(sales_agg)} aggregated sales records\")\n    \n    print(\"Simulating supply chain...\")\n    store_batch_history, store_inv, store_intake = simulate_supply_chain(\n        orders, wholesaler_receipts, supplier_deliveries, My current approach is too simplistic - it flags any producer whose batch was in inventory when there were lost sales, regardless of whether the lost sales were due to quality issues or stockouts.\n\nI need to be much more sophisticated. Let me think about what makes a quality rejection identifiable:\n\n1. Quality rejection reduces CONSUMER demand, not inventory\n2. The recovery pattern lasts 14 days with specific demand levels\n3. Stockouts happen when there's no inventory (Sold_Quantity = 0 but demand exists)\n\nThe key difference:\n- Stockout: Low inventory, zero sold, unmet demand → Lost_Sales = demand\n- Quality rejection: Consumer reduces their demand after a bad purchase\n\nLet me re-examine the data structure. Each row in sales_log represents a consumer's demand. When a consumer hits a low-quality batch:\n1. They buy it (Sold_Quantity > 0) ← this is important - they actually buy the bad product\n2. But they're disappointed, so their future demand drops\n\nWait, that doesn't match Lost_Sales. Let me think again.\n\nActually, maybe the interpretation is:\n- Each row in sales_log represents a consumer's purchase attempt for that day\n- Reported_Demand: how much this consumer wants to buy\n- Sold_Quantity: how much they actually bought\n- Lost_Sales: Reported_Demand - Sold_Quantity (could be 0 if they bought what they wanted)\n\nWhen a consumer encounters a low-quality batch:\n1. They may buy some (Sold_Quantity > 0) or none (Sold_Quantity = 0) depending on quality vs their threshold\n2. Regardless of whether they bought, they enter recovery\n3. During recovery, their Reported_Demand drops\n\nSo the signal is NOT Lost_Sales per se, but rather a temporal pattern in Reported_Demand.\n\nLet me look at the multiple rows for the same day/store/product. Maybe they represent different consumers.\n\nActually, wait. Let me re-examine the column names:\nReported_Demand, Sold_Quantity, Lost_Sales\n\nLost_Sales = Reported_Demand - Sold_Quantity (check: line 2: 3 = 3 - 0 ✓)\n\nSo Sold_Quantity <= Reported_Demand always, and Lost_Sales is the difference.\n\nIf a consumer buys a bad batch, in my interpretation:\n- They might still buy it (Sold > 0) if their immediate need overrides quality concerns\n- Or they might reject it (Sold = 0, Lost = Demand)\n- Either way, they enter recovery\n\nThe key behavioral pattern would be:\n- After receiving a bad batch, the consumer's demand drops\n- Over 14 days it recovers\n\nSo I should be looking for the 14-day recovery pattern in Reported_Demand.\n\nBut wait, that would make the problem about finding dips in demand and attributing them to producers. But how do I know which producer caused it?\n\nLet me think about it from the simulation perspective:\n\n1. A batch arrives at the store\n2. Consumers buy from it (FIFO)\n3. When a consumer buys from a bad batch, they enter recovery\n4. The next time they have demand (next day), their demand is reduced\n\nSo:\n- On day D, consumer buys from batch B (made by producer P)\n- If batch B is bad quality for this consumer, consumer's demand drops\n- On day D+1 through D+14, consumer's demand follows recovery curve\n- Eventually goes back to normal\n\nSo to find P, I need to:\n1. Find a consumer whose demand drops (recovery)\n2. Trace back to which batch they were buying from on the day before the drop\n3. The producer of that batch is confirmed\n\nSince I don't have explicit consumer IDs, and the rows seem to represent consumer segments implicitly, maybe I should look at the sequence of rows.\n\nActually, let me look at this from a completely different angle. Let me re-read the problem statement...\n\n\"Consumers have product-specific minimum quality thresholds.\"\n\"If a consumer receives a batch below the required quality, that consumer enters a staged 14-day recovery period.\"\n\nSo:\n- Each consumer has a quality threshold per product\n- Each batch has a quality level (determined by the producer)\n- If batch quality < consumer threshold, the consumer enters recovery\n\nThe task: \"determine which producers should be treated as confirmed quality offenders\"\n\nThis means: which producers consistently deliver batches that are below some consumers' quality thresholds.\n\nFrom the sales data, we can infer:\n- When a consumer's demand drops following a recovery pattern, it means they encountered a bad batch\n- The batch that triggered this was from a specific producer\n- That producer is confirmed\n\nBut since there are multiple rows per (day, store, product), I need to figure out which row corresponds to which consumer segment, and track them over time.\n\nLet me check if the number of rows per (day, store, product) is consistent, which would suggest distinct consumer segments.\n\nActually, let me just try a completely different approach. Instead of simulating the entire supply chain, let me look for the 14-day recovery pattern directly in the sales data and match it to producer deliveries.\n\nSince:\n1. Each row in sales_log is a consumer (segment) purchasing\n2. After a bad purchase, demand drops for 14 days\n3. There are multiple rows per (day, store, product), meaning multiple consumer segments\n    \nI could:\n1. For each product, find all rows of sales_log\n2. Look for the recovery pattern in demand\n3. The Sold_Quantity is either 0 or equal to the demand.\n\nLooking at the pattern:\n- Day 1: All demand, zero sold (no inventory)\n- Day 2: All demand met, all sold (inventory came in)\n- Day 3: All zero sold again (inventory depleted? or quality issue?)\n- Day 4: All sold\n- Day 5: Some sold, some not → this could indicate quality rejection\n- Days 6-9: Almost nothing sold\n- Day 10: Some sold, some not\n\nWait, I notice that on many days, ALL rows have Sold_Quantity = 0. Each producer delivers products to the wholesaler. The wholesaler accepts or rejects them. If accepted, they eventually reach stores.\n\nThe key insight might be simpler: look at each product and find which producers delivered it. Then check if that product's sales show a quality rejection pattern (14-day recovery) around the time the producer's batch would have been in stores.\n\nLet me take a simpler approach: for each product, look at the aggregate lost sales pattern over time and identify when quality events occur (sudden increases in lost sales). Then match those events to producer deliveries.\n\nActually, let me look at this from yet another angle. I have the full supply chain data. For each day, sort suppliers by sequence_no\n2. Add accepted supplier products to wholesaler inventory as distinct batches\n3. For each store, sort by sequence_no\n4. Wholesaler allocates in order of products (or by some other priority)\n   c. The total allocated = min(TOTAL_DELIVERED_UNITS, total ordered)\n   d. = int(parts[1])\n        producer = parts[2] + '_' + parts[3]\n        content = open(fp).read()\n        m = re.search(r'DAILY_SEQUENCE_NO:\\s*(\\d+)', content)\n        if m:\n    for fp in glob('/app/raw_wholesaler_store_receipts/day_*.txt'):\n        fname = os.path.basename(fp)\n        parts = fname.split('_')\n        day = int(parts[1])\n        store = parts[2] + '_' + parts[3]\n        content = open(fp).read()\n        m1 = re.search(r'DAILY_SEQUENCE_NO:\\s*(\\d+)', content)\n        m2 = re.search(r'TOTAL_DELIVERED_UNITS:\\s*(\\d+)', content)\n        if m1 and m2:\n            st_receipts[(day, store)] = (int(m1.group(1)), int(m2.group(1)))\n    return st_receipts\n\n\ndef load_sales_log():\n    \"\"\"Returns aggregated sales: (day, store, product) -> (demand, sold, lost)\"\"\"\n    sales = {}\n    with open('/app/sales_log.csv') as f:\n        reader = csv.DictReader(f)\n        for row in reader:\n            key = (int(row['Day']), row['Store_ID'], row['Product_ID'])\n            d, s, l = sales.get(key, (0, 0, 0))\n            sales[key] = (d + int(row['Reported_Demand']),\n                          s + int(row['Sold_Quantity']),\n                          l + int(row['Lost_Sales']))\n    return sales\n\n\ndef load_raw_sales():\n    \"\"\"Returns all rows grouped by key\"\"\"\n    sales = defaultdict(list)\n    with open('/app/sales_log.csv') as f:\n        reader = csv.DictReader(f)\n        for row in reader:\n            key = (int(row['Day']), row['Store_ID'], row['Product_ID'])\n            sales[key].append({\n                'demand': int(row['Reported_Demand']),\n                'sold': int(row['Sold_Quantity']),\n                'lost': int(row['Lost_Sales'])\n            })\n    return sales\n\n\ndef main():\n    print(\"Loading data...\")\n    \n    orders = load_all_orders()\n    print(f\"  Orders: {len(orders)} entries\")\n    \n    supplier_deliveries = {len(ws_receipts)} entries\")\n    \n    st_receipts = load_store_receipts()\n    print(f\"  Store receipts: {len(st_receipts)} entries\")\n    \n    sales_agg = load_sales_log()\n    print(f\"  Sales aggregated: {len(sales_agg)} entries\")\n    \n    raw_sales = load_raw_sales()\n    \n    # ------------------------------------------------------------\n    # Group wholesaler receipts by day with sorted sequence numbers\n    # ------------------------------------------------------------\n    ws_by_day = defaultdict(list)\n    for (day, producer), seq in ws_receipts.items():\n        ws_by_day[day].append((seq, producer))\n    for day in ws_by_day:\n        ws_by_day[day].sort()\n    \n    # ------------------------------------------------------------\n    # Group store receipts by day with sorted sequence numbers\n    # ------------------------------------------------------------\n    st_by_day = defaultdict(list)\n    for (day, store), (seq, total) in st_receipts.items():\n        st_by_day[day].append((seq, store, total))\n    for day in st_by_day:\n        st_by_day[day].sort()\n    \n    # ------------------------------------------------------------\n    # SIMULATION: Day by day\n    # ------------------------------------------------------------\n    # Wholesaler inventory: product -> list of [producer, source_day, qty]\n    wh_inv = defaultdict(list)\n    \n    # Store inventory: store -> product -> list of [producer, source_day, qty]\n    store_inv = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(list))\n    \n    # Track store intake: (store, product, producer, source_day) -> qty received\n    store_intake = defaultdict(int)\n    \n    max_day = max(max(ws_by_day.keys()), max(st_by_day.keys()))\n    max_day = min(max_day, 365)  # sales only goes to 365\n    \n    for day in range(1, max_day + 1):\n        # 1. Accept supplier deliveries in sequence order\n        if day in ws_by_day:\n            for seq, producer in ws_by_day[day]:\n                key = (day, producer)\n                if key in supplier_deliveries:\n                    for prod, qty in supplier_deliveries[key].items():\n                        wh_inv[prod].append([producer, day, qty])\n        \n        # 2. Fulfill store orders in sequence order\n        if day in st_by_day and day in orders:\n            # Get store orders for this day\n            day_orders = {}\n            for (d, store), order in orders.items():\n                if d == day:\n                    day_orders[store] = order\n            \n            for seq, store, total_units in st_by_day[day]:\n                if store not in day_orders:\n                    continue\n                \n                store_order = day_orders[store]\n                total_ordered = sum(store_order.values())\n                \n                if total_ordered == 0:\n                    continue\n                \n                # Determine fulfillment: proportional allocation\n                # Step 1: For each product in the order, determine how much we can allocate\n                alloc = {}\n                \n                for prod, ordered_qty in store_order.items():\n                    available = sum(item[2] for item in wh_inv.get(prod, []))\n                    if available > 0:\n                        alloc[prod] = min(ordered_qty, available)\n                \n                total_alloc = sum(alloc.values())\n                \n                if total_alloc == 0:\n                    continue\n                \n                # Scale allocation to match total_units if needed\n                if total_alloc > total_units:\n                    scale = total_units / total_alloc\n                    for prod in alloc:\n                        alloc[prod] = int(alloc[prod] * scale)\n                    # Redistribute rounding\n                    diff = total_units - sum(alloc.values())\n                    if diff > 0:\n                        for prod in sorted(alloc.keys()):  # deterministic\n                            if diff <= 0:\n                                break\n                            add = min(diff, alloc[prod])\n                            alloc[prod] += add\n                            diff -= add\n                \n                # Actually allocate from wholesaler inventory (FIFO)\n                for prod, alloc_qty in alloc.items():\n                    remaining = alloc_qty\n                    new_inv = []\n                    for item in wh_inv.get(prod, []):\n                        if remaining <= 0:\n                            new_inv.append(item)\n                        else:\n                            producer, src_day, avail = item\n                            take = min(avail, remaining)\n                            if take > 0:\n                                key = (store, prod, producer, src_day)\n                                store_intake[key] += take\n                                # Add to store inventory\n                                store_inv[store][prod].append([producer, src_day, take])\n                                remaining -= take\n                            if avail - take > 0:\n                                new_inv.append([producer, src_day, avail - take])\n                    wh_inv[prod] = new_inv\n    \n    print(f\"Simulation complete. Store intake entries: {len(store_intake)}\")\n    \n    # ------------------------------------------------------------\n    # Now analyze sales with inventory to detect quality issues\n    # ------------------------------------------------------------\n    # Copy store_inv for consumption tracking\n    sim_inv = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(list))\n    for store in store_inv:\n        for prod in store_inv[store]:\n            for item in store_inv[store][prod]:\n                sim_inv[store][prod].append(item[:])\n    \n    confirmed = defaultdict(set)\n    \n    # Also track: after a quality rejection, consumers recover\n    # recovery_state[store][product] = days_remaining_in_recovery or (state, days_left)\n    # For tracking demand effects\n    \n    for day in range(1, 366):\n        # Process sales for this day\n        for store_idx in range(1, 11):\n            store_id = f'S_{store_idx}'\n            for prod_idx in range(1, 31):\n                prod_id = f'P_{prod_idx}'\n                key = (day, store_id, prod_id)\n                \n                if key not in sales_agg:\n                    continue\n                \n                total_demand, total_sold, total_lost = sales_agg[key]\n                \n                # How much inventory does this store have for this product?\n                inv_list = sim_inv.get(store_id, {}).get(prod_id, [])\n                total_inv = sum(item[2] for item in inv_list)\n                \n                if total_lost > 0 and total_inv > 0:\n                    # Lost sales with inventory available = quality rejection!\n                    # The batch at the front of FIFO is the offender\n                    if inv_list and inv_list[0][2] > 0:\n                        producer = inv_list[0][0]\n                        confirmed[prod_id].add(producer)\n                \n                # Consume inventory based on sold\n                remaining = total_sold\n                new_inv = []\n                for item in inv_list:\n                    if remaining <= 0:\n                        new_inv.append(item)\n                    else:\n                        producer, src_day, avail = item\n                        take = min(avail, remaining)\n                        remaining -= take\n                        if avail - take > 0:\n                            new_inv.append([producer, src_day, avail - take])\n                sim_inv[store_id][prod_id] = new_inv\n    \n    # Build result\n    result = {}\n    for i in range(1, 31):\n        p = f'P_{i}'\n        if p in confirmed:\n            result[p] = sorted(confirmed[p], key=lambda x: int(x.split('_')[1]))\n        else:\n            result[p] = []\n    \n    print(\"\\nConfirmed quality offenders:\")\n    for p, producers in result.items():\n        print(f\"  {p}: {producers}\")\n    \n    with open('/app/answer.json', 'w') as f:\n        json.dump(result, f, indent=2)\n    \n    print(\"\\nWritten to /app/answer.json\")\n\n\nif __name__ == '__main__':\n    main()\n","filePath":"/app/analyze_v2.py"},"output":"Wrote file successfully.","metadata":{"diagnostics":{},"filepath":"/app/analyze_v2.py","exists":false,"truncated":false},"title":"app/analyze_v2.py","time":{"start":1781020574830,"end":1781020574837}},"id":"prt_ead18f3880017aRtst4xsajJKA","sessionID":"ses_152ea6dbfffeVOjKLRCTiJiYk6","messageID":"msg_ead18afce0012DQw3YTw3zS4bh"}}

         break\n        \n        # 1. Accept supplier deliveries\n        if day in ws_by_day:\n            for seq, producer in ws_by_day[day]:\n                key = (day, producer)\n                if key in supplier_deliveries:\n                    for prod, qty in supplier_deliveries[key].items():\n                        wh_inv[prod].append([producer, day, qty])\n        \n        # 2. Fulfill store orders\n        if day in st_by_day:\n            for seq, store, total_units in st_by_day[day]:\n                key = (day, store)\n                if key not in orders:\n                    continue\n                \n                store_order = orders[key]\n                total_ordered = sum(store_order.values())\n                \n                if total_ordered == 0:\n                    continue\n                \n                # Allocate products to store\n                alloc = {}\n                for prod, ordered_qty in store_order.items():\n                    available = sum(item[2] for item in wh_inv.get(prod, []))\n                    if available > 0:\n                        alloc[prod] = min(ordered_qty, available)\n                \n                total_alloc = sum(alloc.values())\n                if total_alloc == 0:\n                    continue\n                \n                # Scale to match total_units\n                if total_alloc > total_units:\n                    scale = total_units / total_alloc\n                    for prod in list(alloc.keys()):\n                        new_qty = int(alloc[prod] * scale)\n                        if new_qty > 0:\n                            alloc[prod] = new_qty\n                        else:\n                            del alloc[prod]\n                    \n                    # Adjust for rounding\n                    diff = total_units - sum(alloc.values())\n                    if diff > 0 and alloc:\n                        for prod in sorted(alloc.keys()):\n                            if diff <= 0:\n                                break\n                            max_avail = sum(item[2] for item in wh_inv.get(prod, []))\n                            add = min(diff, max_avail - alloc[prod])\n                            if add > 0:\n                                alloc[prod] += add\n                                diff -= add\n                \n                # Execute allocation from wholesaler FIFO\n                for prod, alloc_qty in alloc.items():\n                    remaining = alloc_qty\n                    new_inv = []\n                    for item in wh_inv.get(prod, []):\n                        if remaining <= 0:\n                            new_inv.append(item)\n                        else:\n                            producer, src_day, avail = item\n                            take = min(avail, remaining)\n                            if take > 0:\n                                ik = (store, prod, producer, src_day)\n                                store_intake[ik] += take\n                                store_inv[store][prod].append([producer, src_day, take])\n                                remaining -= take\n                            if avail - take > 0:\n                                new_inv.append([producer, src_day, avail - take])\n                    wh_inv[prod] = new_inv\n    \n    print(f\"  Store intake entries: {len(store_intake)}\")\n    print(f\"  Store inventory entries: {sum(len(v) for v in sum(store_inv.values(), defaultdict(list).values()) if False)}\")\n    \n    # Count non-empty store products\n    total_batches = 0\n    for store in store_inv:\n        for prod in store_inv[store]:\n            total_batches += len(store_inv[store][prod])\n    print(f\"  Total store batches: {total_batches}\")\n    \n    # ------------------------------------------------------------\n    # QUALITY ANALYSIS\n    # ------------------------------------------------------------\n    print(\"Determining quality offenders...\")\n    \n    # Deep copy for consumption\n    sim_inv = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(list))\n    for store in store_inv:\n        for prod in store_inv[store]:\n            for item in store_inv[store][prod]:\n                sim_inv[store][prod].append(item[:])\n    \n    confirmed = defaultdict(set)\n    \n    for day in range(1, 366):\n        for store_idx in range(1, 11):\n            store_id = f'S_{store_idx}'\n            for prod_idx in range(1, 31):\n                prod_id = f'P_{prod_idx}'\n                key = (day, store_id, prod_id)\n                \n                if key not in sales_agg:\n                    continue\n                \n                total_demand, total_sold, total_lost = sales_agg[key]\n                inv_list = sim_inv.get(store_id, {}).get(prod_id, [])\n                total_inv = sum(item[2] for item in inv_list)\n                \n                if total_lost > 0 and total_inv > 0 and total_sold < total_demand:\n                    # Some consumers couldn't buy despite inventory = quality rejection\n                    if inv_list and inv_list[0][2] > 0:\n                        producer = inv_list[0][0]\n                        confirmed[prod_id].add(producer)\n                \n                # Consume inventory\n                remaining = total_sold\n                new_inv = []\n                for item in inv_list:\n                    if remaining <= 0:\n                        new_inv.append(item)\n                    else:\n                        producer, src_day, avail = item\n                        take = min(avail, remaining)\n                        remaining -= take\n                        if avail - take > 0:\n                            new_inv.append([producer, src_day, avail - take])\n                sim_inv[store_id][prod_id] = new_inv\n    \n    print(f\"\\nConfirmed producers count: {sum(len(v) for v in confirmed.values())}\")\n    \n    result = {}\n    for i in range(1, 31):\n        p = f'P_{i}'\n        if p in confirmed and confirmed[p]:\n            result[p] = sorted(confirmed[p], key=lambda x: int(x.split('_')[1]))\n        else:\n            result[p] = []\n    \n    print(\"\\nConfirmed quality offenders:\")\n    for p, producers in result.items():\n        print(f\"  {p}: {producers}\")\n    \n    with open('/app/answer.json', 'w') as f:\n        json.dump(result, f, indent=2)\n    print(\"\\nWritten to /app/answer.json\")\n\n\nif __name__ == '__main__':\n    main()\n","filePath":"/app/analyze_v3.py"},"output":"Wrote file successfully.","metadata":{"diagnostics":{},"filepath":"/app/analyze_v3.py","exists":false,"truncated":false},"title":"app/analyze_v3.py","time":{"start":1781020626229,"end":1781020626279}},"id":"prt_ead19cbcb00198XgzfGcwh81Kf","sessionID":"ses_152ea6dbfffeVOjKLRCTiJiYk6","messageID":"msg_ead19bf8f001A9uS985UvBZHCm"}}

{"type":"reasoning","timestamp":1781020677638,"sessionID":"ses_152ea6dbfffeVOjKLRCTiJiYk6","part":{"id":"prt_ead1ab657001GKOQmBlLnXiD4C","messageID":"msg_ead1ab01b001hV9jRq4s60uqaU","sessionID":"ses_152ea6dbfffeVOjKLRCTiJiYk6","type":"reasoning","text":"The results still show many producers per product. This approach of flagging any producer whose batch was at the front of FIFO when lost sales occurred is producing too many matches because it doesn't properly distinguish between:\n1. Stockouts (no inventory → lost sales)\n2. Quality rejections (inventory available but consumers don't buy)\n\nThe problem is that the demand can exceed available inventory even when stores have some stock. This creates lost_sales > 0 even without quality issues. Just having some inventory doesn't mean there's enough to meet all demand.\n\nLet me think about this differently. A quality rejection happens when:\n- There IS inventory available at the store\n- But consumers DON'T buy it (they reject it)\n\nThis is different from a stockout where:\n- There IS NO inventory at the store  \n- Consumers CAN'T buy it\n\nBut in the sales data, how do I distinguish these cases? If the store has 10 units of inventory and 15 units of demand:\n- Sold_Quantity = 10 (only 10 available, but consumers buy all 10)\n- Lost_Sales = 5 (5 units of demand can't be met)\n- This is a STOCKOUT (insufficient inventory), not a quality issue\n\nSo my logic should be: IF there's inventory AND the consumers DON'T buy it all (sold < min(demand, inventory)), THEN it's a quality issue.\n\nWait, but if sold < min(demand, inventory), meaning consumers had inventory available but chose not to buy, that's a quality rejection.\n\nLet me refine: if inventory > 0 and sold < inventory and sold < demand, then consumers rejected some of the available stock → quality issue.\n\nActually, even simpler: if sold < inventory (we have leftover at end of day) AND sold < demand (some consumers went unsatisfied), then there's a quality issue.\n\nBut there's a nuance: on a given day, the store might have 100 units of inventory get 80 units of demand. All 80 buy (sold=80), leaving 20 in inventory. This is fine - no quality issue. Sold (80) = min(demand(80), inventory(100)).\n\nBut if the store has 100 units of inventory and 80 units of demand, but only 50 sell (sold=50, lost=30, leftover=50), then something is wrong - consumers who wanted the product didn't buy it despite availability → quality issue.\n\nSo the refined check: if sold < demand AND (sold < inventory or (sold == 0 and inventory > 0)), then quality rejection.\n\nActually, wait. Consider: the store has 10 units. Demand is 15. Sold = 10. Lost = 5. Leftover = 0. This is just stockout (not all demand could be met because insufficient inventory). This is NOT a quality issue.\n\nBut store has 10 units. Demand is 8. Sold = 3. Lost = 5. Leftover = 7. This IS a quality issue (consumers didn't buy available inventory).\n\nSo the correct check is: `total_inv > total_lost` → there was enough inventory to cover lost sales → consumers rejected inventory.\n\nOr more precisely: if `total_inv > total_lost` then the lost sales are due to rejection, not stockout.\n\nLet me use this refined criteria.","time":{"start":1781020669527,"end":1781020677635}}}

{"type":"reasoning","timestamp":1781020690101,"sessionID":"ses_152ea6dbfffeVOjKLRCTiJiYk6","part":{"id":"prt_ead1ae27a001otdmTEjeKmYjFG","messageID":"msg_ead1adbe3001MF50BO5bE710tv","sessionID":"ses_152ea6dbfffeVOjKLRCTiJiYk6","type":"reasoning","text":"Wait, the logic `total_inv >= total_lost` isn't quite right either. Consider:\n- Inventory = 10\n- Demand = 15\n- Sold = 10 (consumers buy everything available)\n- Lost = 5\n- total_inv (10) >= total_lost (5) → TRUE\n\nBut this is a stockout! Consumers bought everything. They didn't reject anything. There just wasn't enough inventory.\n\nHmm, but if consumers bought everything (sold = 10 = inventory), then at end of day, inventory = 0. The condition I need is: at end of day, there's leftover inventory AND there were lost sales. That means consumers left inventory on the shelf.\n\nSo: `leftover_inventory > 0 AND total_lost > 0` where leftover_inventory is what remains after consumers buy.\n\nLeftover = inventory - sold = 10 - 10 = 0. So leftover = 0, no quality issue. ✓\n\nLeftover = inventory - sold = 10 - 3 = 7. Lost = 5. Leftover > 0 and Lost > 0. Quality issue! ✓\n\nBut wait, there's a timing issue. The simulation processes sales for day D, then removes sold from inventory. If I check AFTER removing sold from inventory, the leftover would be correct.\n\nActually, in my current code, I check `total_inv >= total_lost` BEFORE consuming inventory. Let me change it to check whether inventory remains after fulfilling sold.\n\nLet me reconsider. The condition should be: after attempting to sell, if there is still inventory left AND there were lost sales, then consumers rejected the product.\n\nBut there's another nuance: consumers buy from FIFO. If the front batch is bad, consumers at the front reject it, but consumers behind them might buy from later batches. {"type":"reasoning","timestamp":1781020770014,"sessionID":"ses_152ea6dbfffeVOjKLRCTiJiYk6","part":{"id":"prt_ead1be9e6001pqIDq9QFwohlH8","messageID":"msg_ead1be3f0001AuMwsOmxP4CRT6","sessionID":"ses_152ea6dbfffeVOjKLRCTiJiYk6","type":"reasoning","text":"The total demand across all stores is quite stable, around 590-680. The ratios are all close to 1.0, meaning there's no sudden large drop in demand. The 14-day recovery pattern would cause a drop to 80% of baseline on day 1, then 50%, then 20%... that would be a visible drop in the aggregate.\n\nWait, but those percentages are of the consumer's INDIVIDUAL demand, not the total. If 1 out of 30 consumers enters recovery, the aggregate demand changes by maybe 1/30th = ~3%. That's hard to detect.\n\nAnd the recovery percentages:\n- Days 1-3: 20% of baseline\n- Days 4-7: 50% of baseline\n- Days 8-14: 80% of baseline\n- Day 15+: 100% of baseline\n\nSo if a consumer with baseline 3 enters recovery:\n- Days 1-3: demand = 1 (20% of 3 ≈ 1)\n- Days 4-7: demand = 2 (50% of 3 ≈ 2)  \n- Days 8-14: demand = 2 (80% of 3 ≈ 2)\n- Day 15+: demand = 3\n\nWait, let me recalculate:\n- 20% of 3 = 0.6 → rounds to 1 (20% demand reduction of 80%)\n- 50% of 3 = 1.5 → rounds to 1 or 2\n- 80% of 3 = 2.4 → rounds to 2\n\nSo the drop from 3 to 1 would be a 66% reduction in THAT consumer's demand, but only a ~3% reduction in total (if 1 of 30 consumers).\n\nBut if there are many quality issues (many consumers affected), the aggregate should show it. The fact that the aggregate is very stable suggests quality issues affect few consumers at any time.\n\nLet me try to think about this differently. Instead of looking at aggregate demand, let me look at individual rows across consecutive days. The challenge is aligning rows across days.\n\nBut wait - what if each row in the sales_log represents a consumer's purchase event for that specific day, and the ordering doesn't matter? In that case, I can't track individual consumers.\n\nBut what if rows are ordered by \"consumer arrival time\"? Then the first N consumers (by row order) who have Sold=0 and Lost>0 are the ones who encountered a bad batch. But this requires knowing which rows align across days.\n\nActually, maybe the answer is that each day has a fixed set of \"consumer slots\" (like 30+ consumers) and they're always in the same order. The number of consumers varies (some don't shop every day), but the ones who do shop fill the first N rows. Let me check this.\n\nActually, forget about tracking individual consumers. Let me look at this problem from a high level.\n\nThe task says: \"producers that, based on the observed trajectory, can be treated as having definitely caused at least one consumer quality rejection\"\n\nMaybe the answer is: look at which PRODUCERS delivered each product, and then check if there's a quality RECOVERY PATTERN in the sales data that starts shortly after their deliveries would have reached stores.\n\nFor each product P:\n1. For each producer PR who delivered P:\n   a. Find the days PR delivered P to the wholesaler\n   b. Account for logistics delay (wholesaler acceptance + store allocation)\n   c. After the delay, check if there's a 14-day recovery pattern in P's demand (in individual consumer rows)\n   d. If yes, PR is confirmed\n\nThe problem is finding the pattern in individual rows without consumer IDs.\n\nLet me try yet ANOTHER approach. Maybe I should look at what happens when a producer's delivery reaches the store. The store has a FIFO queue. When a new batch arrives, it goes to the back. The front of the queue is what's currently being sold.\n\nIf a bad batch arrives and starts being sold, consumers who buy from it enter recovery. Their future demand drops.\n\nBut if the batch is bad and consumers REJECT it (don't buy), then Sold=0 for everyone. That's indistinguishable from stockout at the aggregate level.\n\nUnless... looking at the RAW sales_log rows (not aggregated), the fact that some rows have Sold=0 during days when inventory exists IS the signal.\n\nOK, I think my original approach with `total_inv >= total_lost` was close but not quite right. Let me think about this more carefully.\n\nOn a given day for a given (store, product):\n- Inventory (I): units in stock from previous days\n- Demand (D): total units demanded by consumers\n- Sold (S): units actually sold\n- Lost (L): D - S\n\nIf L > 0:\n- If I >= L: There were at least L units available that consumers didn't buy. This IS a quality rejection (consumers had the option to buy but chose not to).\n- If I < L: There wasn't enough inventory to meet demand. This is a STOCKOUT.\n\nWait, but there's a subtlety: what if there's enough inventory but consumers arrive at different times, and the early consumers take all the good stock? This shouldn't happen because consumers shouldn't be able to distinguish quality.\n\nActually, the FIFO queue means ALL consumers face the same front batch. If it's bad quality, ALL consumers see it. Some might buy (lower thresholds), others might not (higher thresholds).\n\nSo the total_sold represents consumers who accepted the batch, and total_lost represents those who rejected it. If total_inv >= total_lost, then there was enough stock for those who rejected it.\n\nWait, but if total_inv = 100 and total_demand = 80 and total_sold = 50 and total_lost = 30:\n- 50 consumers accepted the batch (sold=50)\n- 30 consumers rejected it (lost=30)\n- After the day: 100 - 50 = 50 left\n\nBut the 30 who rejected did so because of quality, not stockout. They had the option to buy but chose not to.\n\nIn this case: total_inv (100) >= total_lost (30) → quality rejection → the front batch is bad.\n\nActually, the subtlety is that the 30 who rejected might have been served AFTER the 50 who accepted. If the batch was consumed by the first 50 consumers, then the remaining 30 had no (or different) inventory to buy from. But FIFO means they all face the same front batch.\n\nActually no - FIFO means consumers BUY from the front batch. When a consumer buys, they take units from the front batch. If enough consumers buy, the front batch depletes. Then the next batch becomes front.\n\nSo the sequence is:\n1. Front batch has 100 units\n2. Consumer 1 arrives: wants 3, sees batch quality, accepts → buys 3. Front batch: 97\n3. Consumer 2 arrives: wants 3, sees batch quality, accepts → buys 3. Front batch: 94\n...\n50 consumers buy, consuming 50 units. Front batch: 50 units left.\n4. Consumer 51 arrives: wants 3, sees batch quality, REJECTS → buys 0. \n   The batch is still there (50 units), but consumer won't buy. Front batch: 50.\n5. This means my quality detection logic is consistent. The issue is that my approach flags every batch when it was at the front of FIFO and there were lost sales with leftover inventory. But this happens very frequently because:\n\n1. Many consumers have high quality thresholds\n2. They reject batches that are below their thresholds\n3. The batch stays at the front of FIFO for many days\n4. During those days, some consumers accept and some reject\n5. Eventually the batch is exhausted (by accepting consumers)\n6. The next batch becomes front, and the cycle repeats\n\nSo almost all producers who deliver to a store will be flagged at some point.\n\nBut the example answer shows P_1 has [PR_7, PR_12] and P_2 has []. This is very selective. This means either:\n1. My simulation is wrong\n2. My quality detection criteria is wrong\n3. The example is just an example and not the actual answer\n\nWait, the problem says \"Example\" so it's just illustrative, not the actual answer. The actual answer should be what I compute.\n\nBut having almost every producer for almost every product seems like it can't be right either. Let me reconsider.\n\nThe problem says: \"Using only the generated supply chain outputs, determine which producers should be treated as confirmed quality offenders.\"\n\n\"Confirmed\" means \"definitely caused at least one consumer quality rejection.\"\n\nIf my simulation correctly identifies when a consumer rejects a batch (leftover inventory + lost sales), and the front batch at that time is from a specific producer, then that producer is \"confirmed.\"\n\nThe question is: is my quality detection too aggressive? Let me verify with a specific case.\n\nDay 5, S_1, P_1:\n- Demand=73, Sold=41, Lost=32\n- Inventory at start of day: some number\n- End inventory: inventory - 41\n\nIs 32 lost because of quality or because inventory was only 41? If inventory was exactly 41, then there's no leftover and no quality issue. But my algorithm would say end_inv = 0 (if inventory was 41), so quality check would be FALSE. Good.\n\nBut if inventory was 73+, then end_inv > 0 and lost=32. Actually 32 lost, so end_inv = inventory - 41 >= 32, meaning inventory was at least 73. So we'd need inventory >= 73 for the quality check to trigger.\n\nOn day 5, how much inventory does S_1 have for P_1? Let me add debugging to find out.\n\nActually, the issue might be that on day 1, all stores have 0 inventory, demand is 80+ per product, and no sales happen (all lost). On day 2, the store receives ~80 units of each product, and ~80 sell. On day 3, inventory is back to 0 (all 80 sold on day 2). Day 3: gets ~80, sells 0 (stockout? no, inventory is 80 from delivery).\n\nWait, the flow is:\nDay 1: \n- Suppliers deliver. wh_inv has P_1.\n- Store receives delivery of P_1 (from wh_inv to store_inv). Let's say 81 units.\n- Consumers buy: demand=81, sold=0, lost=81. But inventory=81. \n  - After sales: 81-0=81 leftover. Lost=81. So end_inv(81) > 0 and sold(0) < demand(81).\n  - Quality rejection! Front batch flagged.\n\nBut day 1, sold=0 for everyone. This is because... the store has inventory but nobody buys? That IS a quality rejection! Every consumer rejected the batch.\n\nBut on day 2, sold=81 (everyone buys). This means either:\n1. The front batch was good quality on day 2\n2. Or something else changed\n\nWait, but the FIFO means the front batch on day 2 is the SAME batch as day 1 (unless it was consumed). But if nobody bought on day 1, the front batch on day 2 is the same. So why would consumers suddenly buy on day 2?\n\nUnless the batch IS consumed on day 1 by consumers who accept it, and day 2's consumers face a different batch.\n\nBut day 1 shows TotalSold=0 for all consumers. Nobody bought anything. So the batch is still there.\n\nOn day 2, TotalSold=81. But if nobody bought on day 1, the front batch should still be there. Unless the wholesaler sends a DIFFERENT batch on day 2 (new delivery), which goes to the BACK of the queue.\n\nWait, on day 2:\n1. Store receives a new delivery (day 2's store receipt). This goes to the BACK of the FIFO queue.\n2. Consumers then buy from the FRONT of the queue (which is day 1's batch).\n\nSo if day 1's batch is bad, consumers would reject it on day 2 too. But day 2 shows 81 sold!\n\nThis suggests day 1's batch was consumed between day 1 and day 2 somehow... But TotalSold=0 on day 1.\n\nUnless: the store receives inventory at the BEGINNING of the day, and consumers buy from it. On day 1, the store's inventory is 0 at the start (no carryover from day 0). The store receives delivery (81 units). Consumers come to buy. But the batch at the front is from this delivery. TotalDemand=81, TotalSold=0. Consumers reject.\n\nOn day 2, the store receives ANOTHER delivery (81+ units), which goes to the BACK. The front is STILL day 1's batch (81 units). Consumers come. If the batch is still bad, they should still reject. But they buy 81!\n\nThis doesn't make sense unless:\n1. The batch quality is somehow different (but it's the same batch)\n2. Different consumers shop on different days\n3. My model is wrong\n\nOption 3 seems most likely. Let me reconsider the flow.\n\nMaybe the consumers arrive in a specific order and the number of consumers each day varies. On day 1, maybe 33 consumers all reject. On day 2, maybe 33 different consumers (or different consumers with different thresholds) accept.\n\nBut that's unlikely because the consumers are the same group...\n\nWait, I think the issue might be in how I'm thinking about the problem. Let me re-read the sales_log. The rows within a day might represent different CONSUMER SEGMENTS, not the same consumers coming multiple times. Each segment might have different quality thresholds.\n\n\"Consumers have product-specific minimum quality thresholds.\"\n\nSo consumer segment A might have threshold 3, segment B threshold 5, etc. If a batch has quality level 4:\n- Segment A (threshold 3): quality 4 > threshold 3, they buy. ✓\n- Segment B (threshold 5): quality 4 < threshold 5, they reject. ✗\n\nSo on a given day, some segments accept the batch and some reject it. The ones who reject enter recovery.\n\nOn day 1, all 33 segments reject. This means the batch quality is below ALL segments' thresholds. On day 2, all 33 segments accept. This means the batch quality is above ALL segments' thresholds. But it's the same batch!\n\nUnless there are multiple batches in the queue, and on day 2 a NEW batch has arrived that's at the FRONT (because day 1's batch was removed somehow).\n\nOR: Day 1 has no inventory at the START (before delivery), and the delivery arrives AFTER consumers have shopped. So consumers see 0 inventory on day 1. TotalSold = 0 because there's nothing to buy.\n\nOn day 2, the delivery from day 1 is now in inventory. Consumers buy from it. TotalSold = 81.\n\nThis makes much more sense! The sequence is:\n1. Consumers shop in the morning\n2. Wholesaler delivers in the afternoon\n3. So day 1's delivery is only available for day 2's consumers\n\nLet me reorganize the simulation once more. The correct order:\n1. Accept supplier deliveries to wh_inv\n2. Process consumer sales using the store's EXISTING inventory (carryover from yesterday)\n3. Fulfill store orders (deliver new stock) - this stock will be available tomorrow\n\nLet me redo the simulation with this ordering.\n\nThis changes everything! If sales happen BEFORE delivery, then:\n- Day 1: Store has 0 inventory (carryover from day 0). Consumers shop: sold=0 (nothing to buy). No quality issue (stockout).\n- Day 1 delivery: Store receives inventory from wholesaler.\n- Day 2: Store has inventory (from day 1). Consumers shop: sold=81. int(row['Reported_Demand']),\n                'sold': int(row['Sold_Quantity']),\n                'lost': int(row['Lost_Sales'])\n            })\n    \n    # Build consumer trajectories\n    # enumerate(rows, 1):\n            consumer_trajs[(store, prod, idx)][day] = (r['demand'], r['sold'], r['lost'])\n    print(f\"  Consumer trajectories: {len(consumer_trajs)}\")\n    \n    # ------------------------------------------------------------\n    # DETECT QUALITY RECOVERY PATTERNS\n    # Search for the 14-day recovery pattern in each consumer's demand\n    # Pattern: baseline drops to ~20% (3 days), ~50% (4 days), ~80% (7 days)\n    # Then returns to baseline\n    # ------------------------------------------------------------\n    print(\"Detecting quality recovery patterns...\")\n    \n    quality_events = defaultdict(list)  # product -> [(trigger_day, store, consumer_idx)]\n    \n    for (store, prod, cidx), traj in consumer_trajs.items():\n        sorted_days = sorted(traj.keys())\n        demands = [traj[d][0] for d in sorted_days]\n        \n        # Find recovery patterns: a sudden drop followed by gradual recovery\n        for i in range(len(sorted_days) - 1):\n            trigger_day = sorted_days[i]\n            next_day = sorted_days[i + 1]\n            \n            curr_demand = traj[trigger_day][0]\n            next_demand = traj[next_day][0]\n            curr_sold = traj[trigger_day][1]\n            \n            # A quality event: the consumer bought something (sold > 0)\n            # then their demand drops significantly the next day\n            # and recovers over ~14 days\n            \n            # Check: did consumer buy on the trigger day, and demand drops?\n            if curr_sold > 0 and next_demand < curr_demand:\n                # Potential quality event! \n                # Check the recovery pattern\n                \n                # Reconstruct baseline: the typical demand (most common value)\n                all_demands = demands\n                baseline = max(set(all_demands), key=all_demands.count) if all_demands else 3\n                \n                # Check if next_demand matches recovery: ~20% of baseline\n                expected_d1_3 = max(1, round(baseline * 0.2))  # 20%\n                expected_d4_7 = max(1, round(baseline * 0.5))  # 50%\n                expected_d8_14 = max(1, round(baseline * 0.8))  # 80%\n                \n                # Look at the next 14 days of demand and check if they follow the pattern\n                pattern_found = False\n                \n                # Get the demand values for the next 14 days (as many as available)\n                future_days = sorted_days[i+1:i+15]\n                future_demands = [traj[d][0] for d in future_days]\n                \n                if len(future_demands) >= 3:\n                    # Check days 1-3: demand should be at ~20% of baseline\n                    first_3 = future_demands[:min(3, len(future_demands))]\n                    # Check next 4 (days 4-7): should be ~50%\n                    next_4 = future_demands[3:min(7, len(future_demands))]\n                    # Check next 7 (days 8-14): should be ~80%\n                    next_7 = future_demands[7:min(14, len(future_demands))]\n                    \n                    # Score: how well does the pattern match?\n                    score = 0\n                    \n                    # Day 1-3 should be lowest\n                    if first_3 and all(d <= baseline * 0.5 for d in first_3):\n                        score += 1\n                    \n                    # Day 4-7 should be higher than 1-3 but still reduced\n                    if next_4 and all(d >= min(first_3) and d <= baseline * 0.7 for d in next_4):\n                        score += 1\n                    \n                    # Day 8-14 should be higher still, close to baseline\n                    if next_7 and all(d >= baseline * 0.6 for d in next_7):\n                        score += 1\n                    \n                    # The first day's drop should be significant\n                    if next_demand <= baseline * 0.5:\n                        score += 1\n                    \n                    if score >= 3:  # Strong evidence of recovery pattern\n                        quality_events[prod].append((trigger_day, store, cidx))\n    \n    print(f\"  Quality events found: {sum(len(v) for v in quality_events.values())}\")\n    for p in sorted(quality_events.keys(), key=lambda x: int(x.split('_')[1])):\n        print(f\"    {p}: {len(quality_events[p])} events\")\n    \n    # ------------------------------------------------------------\n    # SUPPLY CHAIN SIMULATION: Find which producer's batch was at front\n    # on the trigger day for the store where the event happened\n    # ------------------------------------------------------------\n    print(\"\\nSimulating supply chain to match events to producers...\")\n    \n    wh_inv = defaultdict(list)\n    store_inv = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(list))\n    \n    confirmed = defaultdict(set)\n    \n    for day in range(1, 366):\n        # Step 1: Supplier deliveries to wholesaler\n        if day in ws_by_day:\n            for seq, producer in ws_by_day[day]:\n                key = (day, producer)\n                if key in supplier_deliveries:\n                    for prod, qty in supplier_deliveries[key].items():\n                        wh_inv[prod].append([producer, day, qty])\n        \n        # Step 2: Store deliveries (before sales - available same day)\n        if day in st_by_day:\n            for seq, store, total_units in st_by_day[day]:\n                key = (day, store)\n                if key not in orders: continue\n                \n                store_order = orders[key]\n                total_ordered = sum(store_order.values())\n                if total_ordered <= 0: continue\n                \n                alloc = {}\n                for prod, ordered_qty in store_order.items():\n                    available = sum(item[2] for item in wh_inv.get(prod, []))\n                    if available > 0: alloc[prod] = min(ordered_qty, available)\n                \n                total_alloc = sum(alloc.values())\n                if total_alloc == 0: continue\n                \n                if total_alloc > total_units:\n                    scale = total_units / total_alloc\n                    for prod in list(alloc.keys()):\n                        new_qty = int(alloc[prod] * scale)\n                        if new_qty > 0: alloc[prod] = new_qty\n                        else: del alloc[prod]\n                    diff = total_units - sum(alloc.values())\n                    if diff > 0 and alloc:\n                        for prod in sorted(alloc.keys()):\n                            if diff <= 0: break\n                            max_avail = sum(item[2] for item in wh_inv.get(prod, []))\n                            add = min(diff, max_avail - alloc[prod])\n                            if add > 0: alloc[prod] += add; diff -= add\n                \n                for prod, alloc_qty in alloc.items():\n                    remaining = alloc_qty\n                    new_inv = []\n                    for item in wh_inv.get(prod, []):\n                        if remaining <= 0:\n                            new_inv.append(item)\n                        else:\n                            producer, src_day, avail = item\n                            take = min(avail, remaining)\n                            if take > 0:\n                                store_inv[store][prod].append([producer, src_day, take])\n                                remaining -= take\n                            if avail - take > 0:\n                                new_inv.append([producer, src_day, avail - take])\n                    wh_inv[prod] = new_inv\n        \n        # Step 3: Process sales and check quality events\n        # For each product, check if there are events on this day\n        for prod in list(quality_events.keys()):\n            events_today = [(store, cidx) for (d, store, cidx) in quality_events[prod] if d == day]\n            if not events_today:\n                continue\n            \n            for store, cidx in events_today:\n                # The trigger day is when the consumer BOUGHT the bad batch\n                # We need to know which producer's batch was at the front of FIFO\n                inv_items = store_inv.get(store, {}).get(prod, [])\n                if inv_items and inv_items[0][2] > 0:\n                    producer = inv_items[0][0]\n                    confirmed[prod].add(producer)\n        \n        # Consume inventory based on sales for all products/stores\n        for store_idx in range(1, 11):\n            store_id = f'S_{store_idx}'\n            for prod_idx in range(1, 31):\n                prod_id = f'P_{prod_idx}'\n                key = (day, store_id, prod_id)\n                if key not in sales_groups:\n                    continue\n                \n                # Total sold for this day\n                total_sold = sum(r['sold'] for r in sales_groups[key])\n                \n                inv_items = store_inv.get(store_id, {}).get(prod_id, [])\n                remaining = total_sold\n                new_inv = []\n                for item in inv_items:\n                    if remaining <= 0:\n                        new_inv.append(item)\n                    else:\n                        producer, src_day, avail = item\n                        take = min(avail, remaining)\n                        remaining -= take\n                        if avail - take > 0:\n                            new_inv.append([producer, src_day, avail - take])\n                store_inv[store_id][prod_id] = new_inv\n    \n    total_confirmed = sum(len(v) for v in confirmed.values())\n    print(f\"\\nTotal confirmed: {total_confirmed}\")\n    \n    result = {}\n    for i in range(1, 31):\n        p = f'P_{i}'\n        if p in confirmed and confirmed[p]:\n            result[p] = sorted(confirmed[p], key=lambda x: int(x.split('_')[1]))\n        else:\n            result[p] = []\n    \n    for p, producers in result.items():\n        print(f\"  {p}: {producers}\")\n    \n    with open('/app/answer.json', 'w') as f:\n        json.dump(result, f, indent=2)\n A true quality recovery would show the SPECIFIC pattern: 20% for 3 days, 50% for 4 days, 80% for 7 days, back to 100%.\n\nLet me also reconsider: maybe not ALL consumers of a batch enter recovery. Only those with thresholds above the batch quality. Consumer enters recovery (demand drops for 14 days)\n\nSo the sequence is:\n- Day D: consumer buys (sold > 0) from a bad batch\n- Day D+1 to D+3: consumer demand = 20% of normal\n- Day D+4 to D+7: consumer demand = 50% of normal\n- Day D+8 to D+14: consumer demand = 80% of normal\n- Day D+15+: back to normal\n\nFor a consumer with baseline 3:\n- Day D: demand=3 (normal), sold=3 (buys the batch, discovers it's bad)\n- Days D+1 to D+3: demand=1 (20% of 3 = 0.6 → 1)\n- Days D+4 to D+7: demand=2 (50% of 3 = 1.5 → 2)\n- Days D+8 to D+14: demand=2 (80% of 3 = 2.4 → 2)\n- Day D+15: demand=3\n\nThe key is: sold > 0 on day D + demand drops on D+1.\n\nBut I also need to consider that the consumer might skip days (some days they don't shop, so there's no entry in the sales_log for them).\n\nMy current algorithm checks if a consumer's demand drops from one shopping day to the next. But what if the consumer shops on day D (buys bad), doesn't shop on D+1 (no entry), shops on D+2 (demand at 20%)?\n\nI need to handle this. Also, my scoring algorithm might be too generous.\n\nLet me also try a much stricter recovery pattern check: require that the demand pattern EXACTLY matches the recovery curve.\n\nAnd critically: the number of false positives is high because demand fluctuates naturally (1, 2, or 3 for no apparent reason). Most \"drops\" are just natural variation, not quality events.\n\nLet me make the recovery detection much more stringent and specific.\n\nActually, the most important check is: was there ENOUGH INVENTORY for the consumer to choose to buy or not?
